title: Highlights from Ilya Sutskever's November 2025 interview ...
excerpts:
Section Title: Effective Altruism Forum > Background
Content:
[Ilya Sutskever](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ilya_Sutskever) is a renowned machine learning researcher who co-authored the [AlexNet](https://computerhistory.org/blog/chm-releases-alexnet-source-code/) paper in 2012 that helped kick off the [deep learning revolution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deep_learning) . Sutskever co-founded OpenAI and served as Chief Scientist until 2024. He participated in the temporary ouster of CEO Sam Altman and subsequently left the company. Following his departure, he founded his own company, [Safe Superintelligence](https://www.reuters.com/technology/artificial-intelligence/openai-co-founder-sutskevers-new-safety-focused-ai-startup-ssi-raises-1-billion-2024-09-04/) , which has [raised](https://www.crunchbase.com/discover/funding_rounds/field/organizations/funding_total/safe-superintelligence) $3 billion, but about which little else is known.

title: AI and connectionism (Ilya Sutskever - NeurIPS 2024)
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > A deep dive into Ilya Sutskevers claims on AI and connectionism with chatGPT.
Content:
```
**Detailed Overview of Biological Inspirations and Connectionism in Ilya Sutskever’s Talk** 

Sutskever delved into the role of biological inspiration in AI development, emphasizing the importance of connectionism as a guiding principle for deep learning. His key points included:

 **The Case for Connectionism** 
-  **Definition of Connectionism** : Sutskever framed connectionism as the idea that artificial neurons are loosely analogous to biological neurons. While not identical, this similarity has provided a foundation for the confidence in scaling neural networks. -  **Biological Analogy** : He argued that if one accepts that artificial neurons are “kind of, sort of like” biological neurons, then large neural networks could, in principle, replicate many human cognitive capabilities. He highlighted this belief as the basis for deploying large neural networks.

title: Ilya Sutskever's research works | The Graduate Center, CUNY and other places

Content:
... For instance, CLIP [10] aligns textual and visual features within the same semantic space through a contrastive learning framework, enabling the model to capture the relationships between text and visual content. Other multimodal models, such as Glide [11] and VilBERT [12], have been employed in tasks like text-to-image generation and cross-modal representation learning. The features encoded by these multimodal models inherently exhibit strong correlations across modalities, which facilitates the extraction of task-relevant information during the fusion process. ...Reference:Multi-grained feature gating fusion network for multimodal sentiment analysis

title: Ilya Sutskever Declares the Scaling Era Dead. His $3 Billion Bet Says Research Will Win.
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > Ilya Sutskever helped prove that scale works in AI. Now he says it doesn't—and his $3 billi...
Content:
Ilya Sutskever thinks everyone is doing AI wrong. Not just slightly wrong. Fundamentally, paradigmatically wrong.
In a recent interview with Dwarkesh Patel, the co-founder of Safe Superintelligence (SSI) and former OpenAI chief scientist laid out a thesis that should unsettle every frontier lab executive currently signing billion-dollar compute contracts. The age of scaling, he argues, has ended. What comes next requires something the industry has largely abandoned: actual research.
"We got to the point where we are in a world where there are more companies than ideas," Sutskever observed. "By quite a bit."
The statement lands differently coming from him. This is the researcher who co-authored AlexNet, helped build GPT-3, and spent a decade proving that scale works. Now he's saying it doesn't. At least, not anymore.

title: Ilya Sutskever
excerpts: **Ilya Sutskever** ( Hebrew : איליה סוצקבר ; born 1986) is a computer scientist who specializes in machine learning . <sup>[[ 4 ]]()</sup> He has made several major contributions to the field of deep learning , including sequence-to-sequence learning , reasoning models , GPT models, and contributions to CLIP , DALL-E , and AlphaGo . <sup>[[ 5 ]](:0-5)</sup> With Alex Krizhevsky and Geoffrey Hinton , he co-created AlexNet , a convolutional neural network . <sup>[[ 6 ]]()</sup> One of the most highly cited computer scientists in history, he has won the NeurIPS Test of Time Award for his lasting impact on AI research three times in a row (2022-2024) and received the National Academy of Sciences Award for the Industrial Application of Science in 2026. <sup>[[ 7 ]]()</sup> <sup>[[ 3 ]]()</sup>

title: Speaker Details: EmTech AI 2026
excerpts:
Section Title: Speaker Details
Content:
**Full Name**
Ilya Sutskever
**Job Title**
Co-Founder & Chief Scientist
**Company**
OpenAI
**Brief Biography**
Ilya Sutskever is Cofounder and Chief Scientist of OpenAI, which aims to build artificial general intelligence that benefits all of humanity. He leads research at OpenAI and is one of the architects behind the GPT models.
Prior to OpenAI, Ilya was co-inventor of AlexNet and Sequence to Sequence Learning, as well as AlphaGo and TensorFlow. He earned his BSc, MSc, and PhD in Computer Science from the University of Toronto.
[Close](https://event.technologyreview.com/emtech-ai-2026)

title: The Ilya Sutskever Journey: From Theoretical Foundations ...
excerpts: Sutskever's contributions to OpenAI's technical stack demonstrate several key principles for AI system development: Scalable Training

title: Why the Architects of Modern AI Do Not Think LLMs Lead to AGI
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > Ilya and the need to push past passive learning
Content:
**Sutskever’s view is notable because it comes from someone who helped define the era of scaling. His argument is** ***not that large language models are a dead end*** **, but that they are an incomplete foundation. In his view, reaching AGI will** ***require moving beyond passive learning*** **toward systems that interact with the world, generate their own experiences, and reason in deeper and more reliable ways.**

title: The wacky beliefs of the tech elite shaping global society
excerpts:
Employees at the company, which makes ChatGPT, also claimed Sutskever led ritualistic chants of "Free the AGI," referring to Artificial General Intelligence, which can think for itself like a human, before he left the company in 2024.
OpenAI co-founder Ilya Sutskever has been portrayed by colleagues as an esoteric spiritual leader obsessed with superpowered AIs who [burns wooden effigies](https://futurism.com/openai-employees-say-firms-chief-scientist-has-been-making-strange-spiritual-claims) to "unaligned AIs" at holiday parties and team building retreats.
OpenAI co-founder Ilya Sutskever has been portrayed by colleagues as an esoteric spiritual leader who is obsessed with superpowered AIs and burns wooden effigies to "unaligned AIs" at holiday parties and team-building retreats. Getty Images for SSI 11

title: What's Next for AI Systems & Language Models With Ilya ...
excerpts:
Section Title: What's Next for AI Systems & Language Models With Ilya Sutskever of OpenAI > TRANSCRIPT
Content:
Ilya Sutskever (38:33):
Yeah, I'd say it's definitely a true statement that the field of deep learning, especially the academic branch, not so much the applied branch, but the academic branch of the field has underestimated the importance of data because of the mental framework of the data is given to you in the form of a benchmark and your goal is to create a better method that is better than the other existing methods.
Ilya Sutskever (39:03):
And the reason it was important for that framework to have a fixed data set is so that you could compare which method is better. And I think that really did lead to a blind spot where very many researchers were working very hard on this pretty difficult area of can we improve the model more and more and more, while leaving with a very large improvements that are possible by simply saying, "Hey, let's get much more data on the table."
